news_summary: Scientists have made a groundbreaking discovery of ice clouds on a giant alien planet using the James Webb Space Telescope, revealing new insights into extraterrestrial atmospheres.
news_summary_fr: Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb, des scientifiques ont fait une découverte révolutionnaire : la présence de nuages de glace sur une planète extraterrestre géante, ce qui apporte un nouvel éclairage sur les atmosphères extraterrestres.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han realizado un descubrimiento revolucionario de nubes de hielo en un gigantesco planeta extraterrestre gracias al telescopio espacial James Webb, lo que ha aportado nuevos conocimientos sobre las atmósferas extraterrestres.

articles.title: Scientists stunned as JWST finds ice clouds on a giant alien planet
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered unexpected water-ice clouds on a distant, Jupiter-like exoplanet, challenging current atmospheric models. By directly imaging Epsilon Indi Ab with the James Webb Space Telescope, they found less ammonia than expected—likely hidden by thick, patchy clouds. The finding reveals new layers of complexity in giant planets and shows how much we still have to learn.
articles.content:
Astronomers have identified something surprising on a distant gas giant: water ice clouds. The discovery was made by a team led by Elisabeth Matthews at the Max Planck Institute for Astronomy (MPIA), and it challenges many existing models of how exoplanet atmospheres behave. The planet, known as Epsilon Indi Ab, is similar to Jupiter, yet its atmosphere appears more complex than expected. The observing method used in this study also marks an important step toward the long term goal of finding and studying Earth like planets.

The search for planets beyond our solar system has evolved over decades. Scientists ultimately hope to detect signs of life on distant worlds, possibly within the next few decades. Early efforts, from 1995 through about 2022, focused mainly on discovering new exoplanets. Researchers relied on indirect techniques that could reveal a planet's mass, size, or sometimes both.

The launch of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) in 2022 marked the beginning of a new phase. For the first time, astronomers could study the atmospheres of many exoplanets in detail, gaining insights into their composition and structure. Even so, this stage is still a step away from directly searching for life, which will likely require more advanced telescopes in the future.

The latest research pushes these techniques further, although it does not yet target Earth like planets. Elisabeth Matthews (Max Planck Institute for Astronomy), the study's lead author, explains: "JWST is finally allowing us to study solar-system analogue planets in detail. If we were aliens, several light years away, and looking back at the Sun, JWST is the first telescope that would allow us to study Jupiter in detail. For studying Earth in detail, we would need much more advanced telescopes, though."

Why Jupiter Like Exoplanets Are Hard to Study

Despite JWST's capabilities, studying planets similar to Jupiter has been difficult. Most gas giants observed so far are much hotter than Jupiter. This is because the most common method of studying exoplanet atmospheres requires the planet to pass in front of its star from Earth's perspective. Planets closer to their stars are more likely to align this way, but they are also much hotter.

To get around this limitation, Matthews and her team used a different approach. Their work provides one of the closest looks yet at a true Jupiter analogue, and it revealed an unexpected feature.

Using JWST's mid infrared instrument MIRI, the team directly imaged Epsilon Indi Ab. This planet orbits the star Epsilon Indi A in the constellation Indus (in the southern sky). According to Bhavesh Rajpoot, a PhD student at MPIA who contributed to the research, "This planet has a considerably greater mass than Jupiter -- the new study fixes its mass at 7.6 Jupiter masses -- but the diameter is about the same as for its solar-system cousin."

A Cold Giant With Lingering Heat

Epsilon Indi Ab orbits about four times farther from its star than Jupiter does from the Sun. Its host star is slightly smaller and cooler than the Sun, which keeps the planet's temperature relatively low. Its surface temperature is estimated to be between 200 and 300 Kelvin (between -70 and +20 degrees Celsius).

Even so, the planet is warmer than Jupiter, which has a temperature of about 140 K. Scientists believe this extra warmth comes from heat left over from the planet's formation. Over billions of years, Epsilon Indi Ab is expected to cool and eventually become even colder than Jupiter.

To observe the planet, astronomers used a coronagraph on the MIRI instrument to block out the bright light from the host star. This allowed them to detect the faint glow of the planet itself. They captured images using a filter at 11.3 μm, which sits just outside a wavelength associated with ammonia molecules NH3. By comparing these observations with earlier images taken at 10.6 μm in 2024, the team was able to estimate how much ammonia is present. (Incidentally, both the mechanical filter wheels placing the coronagraph and the filter in front of the MIRI camera were constructed at MPIA, one of the German contributions to the JWST.)

Evidence Points to Water Ice Clouds

In Jupiter's atmosphere, ammonia gas and ammonia clouds dominate the visible upper layers. Based on its properties, Epsilon Indi Ab was expected to contain large amounts of ammonia gas as well, but not ammonia clouds. Instead, the observations revealed less ammonia than predicted.

The most likely explanation is the presence of thick but uneven water ice clouds, similar to cirrus clouds high in Earth's atmosphere -- an unexpected complication.

Astronomers typically interpret such data by comparing observations with computer models of planetary atmospheres. However, many existing models do not include clouds because they are difficult to simulate. This discovery highlights the need to improve those models. James Mang (University of Texas at Austin), a co author of the study, notes: "It's a great problem to have, and it speaks to the immense progress we're making thanks to JWST. What once seemed impossible to detect is now within reach, allowing us to probe the structure of these atmospheres, including the presence of clouds. This reveals new layers of complexity that our models are now beginning to capture, and opens the door to even more detailed characterization of these cold, distant worlds."

Looking Ahead With Future Telescopes

Future observations could provide even clearer views of these clouds. NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, where MPIA is a partner, is expected to launch in 2026-2027 and should be well suited for directly detecting reflective water ice clouds.

In the meantime, Matthews and her colleagues are seeking additional JWST observation time to study more cold Jupiter like planets. As researchers continue refining their techniques, they are building the foundation for studying Earth like worlds in the future and, ultimately, searching for signs of life beyond our solar system.

Background Information

The results described here have been published as E. C. Matthews et al., "A second visit to Eps Ind Ab with JWST: new photometry confirms ammonia and suggests thick clouds in the exoplanet atmosphere of the closest super-Jupiter" in the Astrophysical Journal Letters.

The MPIA researchers involved are Elisabeth Matthews and Bhavesh Rajpoot, in collaboration with James Mang and Caroline Morley (University of Texas at Austin), Aarynn Carter and Mathilde Mâlin (Space Telescope Science Institute), and others.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of water ice clouds on a Jupiter-like exoplanet using the James Webb Space Telescope, which challenges existing atmospheric models and advances our understanding of exoplanet atmospheres. This breakthrough has broad implications for future studies of Earth-like planets and the search for life beyond our solar system, representing a meaningful step forward in space exploration and planetary science.
articles.personality_title: James Webb Telescope discovers water ice clouds on distant Jupiter-like planet
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets, to learn about their atmospheres and look for signs of life. The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), launched in 2022, helps astronomers see these planets in more detail than ever before.

**What happened** – A team led by Elisabeth Matthews used JWST to take direct pictures of a giant planet named Epsilon Indi Ab, which is similar to Jupiter but much farther away. They found something surprising: thick, patchy clouds made of water ice, instead of the expected ammonia gas. This was discovered by blocking the star’s bright light and studying the planet’s glow in infrared light.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that giant planets can have more complex atmospheres than scientists thought. It challenges current models that don’t fully include clouds, especially ice clouds. Understanding these clouds helps scientists learn how giant planets form and change over time. It also improves the techniques needed to study planets more like Earth in the future.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to use JWST more to observe other cold, Jupiter-like planets and study their atmospheres. Upcoming telescopes like NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, launching around 2026, will help detect clouds even better. These efforts build toward the goal of finding Earth-like planets and possibly signs of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope, scientists found unexpected water ice clouds on a distant giant planet, revealing new details about exoplanet atmospheres and guiding future space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb découvre des nuages de glace d'eau sur une planète géante lointaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, pour mieux comprendre leurs atmosphères et chercher des signes de vie. Le télescope spatial James Webb (JWST), lancé en 2022, permet aux astronomes d’observer ces planètes avec un niveau de détail inédit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Elisabeth Matthews a utilisé le JWST pour prendre des images directes d’une planète géante nommée Epsilon Indi Ab, semblable à Jupiter mais bien plus éloignée. Ils ont découvert quelque chose de surprenant : des nuages épais et irréguliers de glace d’eau, au lieu du gaz d’ammoniac attendu. Cette découverte a été possible en bloquant la lumière brillante de l’étoile et en étudiant la lumière infrarouge émise par la planète.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les atmosphères des planètes géantes sont plus complexes que ce que pensaient les scientifiques. Elle remet en question les modèles actuels qui ne prennent pas bien en compte les nuages, surtout ceux de glace. Comprendre ces nuages aide à mieux connaître la formation et l’évolution des grandes planètes. Cela améliore aussi les méthodes pour étudier à l’avenir des planètes plus semblables à la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent utiliser davantage le JWST pour observer d’autres planètes froides similaires à Jupiter et analyser leurs atmosphères. Le futur télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA, prévu pour 2026, permettra de détecter encore mieux ces nuages. Ces progrès préparent la voie pour trouver des planètes semblables à la Terre et peut-être des signes de vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce au télescope James Webb, des scientifiques ont découvert des nuages de glace d’eau inattendus sur une planète géante lointaine, révélant de nouveaux détails sur les atmosphères des exoplanètes et orientant l’exploration spatiale future.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio James Webb descubre nubes de hielo de agua en un planeta gigante lejano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas, para entender sus atmósferas y buscar señales de vida. El telescopio espacial James Webb (JWST), lanzado en 2022, permite a los astrónomos observar estos planetas con mucho más detalle.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Elisabeth Matthews usó el JWST para tomar imágenes directas de un planeta gigante llamado Epsilon Indi Ab, parecido a Júpiter pero mucho más distante. Encontraron algo sorprendente: nubes gruesas y desiguales hechas de hielo de agua, en lugar del gas amoníaco que esperaban. Esto se descubrió bloqueando la luz brillante de la estrella y analizando el brillo infrarrojo del planeta.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que las atmósferas de los planetas gigantes son más complejas de lo que pensaban los científicos. Desafía los modelos actuales que no incluyen bien las nubes, especialmente las de hielo. Entender estas nubes ayuda a aprender cómo se forman y cambian estos planetas grandes. También mejora las técnicas para estudiar en el futuro planetas más parecidos a la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren usar más el JWST para observar otros planetas fríos similares a Júpiter y estudiar sus atmósferas. El próximo telescopio espacial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA, que se lanzará alrededor de 2026, ayudará a detectar mejor estas nubes. Estos avances preparan el camino para encontrar planetas parecidos a la Tierra y posiblemente señales de vida.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando el telescopio James Webb, los científicos encontraron nubes inesperadas de hielo de agua en un planeta gigante lejano, revelando nuevos detalles sobre las atmósferas de exoplanetas y guiando la exploración espacial futura.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, detailed painting of a massive gas giant planet with swirling, translucent ice clouds drifting high in its atmosphere, illuminated softly by a distant star; nearby, a sleek, symbolic telescope silhouette with a glowing lens points toward the planet against a backdrop of deep space in warm, muted earth tones.
